skill.name: backend-patterns
skill.description: Backend architecture patterns, API design, database optimization, and server-side best practices for Node.js, Express, and Next.js API routes.
skill.summary: Comprehensive backend development patterns including API design, repository pattern, caching, authentication, and error handling.
skill.author: affaan-m

security_audit.summary: Security evaluation complete. All 58 static findings are false positives. The skill contains legitimate backend development patterns with code examples demonstrating proper security practices including JWT authentication, RBAC, rate limiting, and environment variable handling. No malicious behavior detected.

content.value_statement: Learn battle-tested backend architecture patterns for scalable Node.js applications with examples for API design, database optimization, caching, authentication, and error handling.
actual_capabilities: Implement RESTful API design with proper URL structure and HTTP methods
Apply repository pattern for clean data access layer separation
Create service layer to encapsulate business logic
Implement JWT authentication with role-based access control
Add Redis caching with cache-aside pattern for performance
Handle errors centrally with proper HTTP status codes
limitations: Skill focuses on patterns rather than complete implementation details
Does not cover specific database vendor configurations
Requires understanding of TypeScript and Node.js basics
Rate limiter example is basic; production systems may need Redis-based solution

use_cases.title: Building New API Endpoints
use_cases.description: Use RESTful patterns to design clean, consistent API endpoints with proper HTTP methods and status codes.

use_cases.title: Optimizing Database Performance
use_cases.description: Apply query optimization and N+1 prevention patterns to reduce database load and improve response times.

use_cases.title: Adding Authentication to APIs
use_cases.description: Implement JWT token validation and role-based access control for secure API endpoints.
use_cases.target_user: Full-stack developers adding auth to applications

prompt_templates.title: Basic API Structure
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to structure a RESTful API with GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, and DELETE endpoints for a resource like 'products'.

prompt_templates.title: Database Query Optimization
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a TypeScript example showing how to prevent N+1 queries when fetching a list of orders with their associated customers.

prompt_templates.title: JWT Authentication Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a JWT authentication middleware for Next.js API routes that validates tokens and attaches user info to the request.

prompt_templates.title: Redis Caching Layer
prompt_templates.prompt: Implement a cache-aside pattern using Redis to cache database query results with a 5-minute TTL.

output_examples.input: Show me how to implement JWT authentication with role-based access control
output_examples.output: Code example showing JWT verification, token extraction from headers, role permissions mapping, and middleware for checking user permissions like 'read', 'write', 'delete', 'admin'.

output_examples.input: How do I structure a Node.js API with proper error handling?
output_examples.output: ApiError class with statusCode and message, centralized error handler that catches ApiError and ZodError, returns proper JSON responses with appropriate HTTP status codes.

output_examples.input: What patterns help prevent N+1 database queries?
output_examples.output: Batch fetch pattern: collect all IDs first, single query to fetch all related records, map results using a HashMap for O(1) lookups instead of N individual queries.

best_practices: Separate concerns using repository pattern for data access and service layer for business logic
Always validate input using libraries like Zod before processing
Use environment variables for secrets like JWT_SECRET, never hardcode credentials
Implement rate limiting to protect APIs from abuse and DoS attacks
anti_patterns: Avoid selecting all columns with SELECT * when you only need specific fields
Do not make database queries inside loops; batch fetch instead
Never expose raw database errors to clients; always return generic error messages
Avoid storing sensitive data in JWT tokens; only include non-secret claims like userId and role

faq.question: What is the repository pattern?
faq.answer: The repository pattern creates an abstract data access layer. It defines methods like findAll, findById, create, update, and delete that hide the details of how data is fetched or stored, making your code testable and flexible.

faq.question: How does the service layer differ from the repository?
faq.answer: The service layer contains business logic and orchestrates multiple repository calls. The repository only handles data access. This separation makes your code easier to test and maintain.

faq.question: What is the cache-aside pattern?
faq.answer: Cache-aside means checking the cache first. If the data exists, return it. If not, fetch from the database, store in cache, then return. This pattern reduces database load for frequently accessed data.

faq.question: How do I handle errors in Express/Next.js APIs?
faq.answer: Create a custom error class with statusCode, implement a centralized error handler middleware, catch errors and return appropriate HTTP status codes (400 for validation, 401 for auth, 500 for server errors).

faq.question: What is N+1 query problem?
faq.answer: N+1 happens when you fetch N records then make N separate queries to get related data for each record. Solution: batch fetch all related records in one query then map them in memory.

faq.question: How should I store JWT secrets?
faq.answer: Always store JWT secrets in environment variables like process.env.JWT_SECRET. Never hardcode secrets in your source code. Use strong, random secrets at least 256 bits long.
